campaigns.name: PA Outreach — CFO / VP Finance / FP&A

emails.subject: Your forecasting cycle doesn't have to take 80+ hours
emails.body: Hi [First Name], I work with finance teams across the region who are spending weeks in spreadsheets just to get quarterly forecasts locked down. A lot of them tell me the same thing: the data's there, but pulling it together manually eats up their whole month. I noticed you're leading FP&A at [Company], and I thought you might relate. We recently helped a forest products company cut their forecasting time from 80+ hours down to under 15—mostly by automating the pattern-finding work Excel can't do at scale. Would you be open to a quick 15-minute call this week? Happy to walk through how it works, no pressure. Bradley Milks IBM Account Executive, Data Trust & Planning bradley.milks@ibm.com

emails.subject: Your forecast accuracy gap (and why it matters for budget variance)
emails.body: Hi [First Name], Following up on my note last week—I wanted to highlight something specific to your role. Most finance teams we talk to discover their forecast accuracy actually drops during peak planning cycles, when they're manually juggling the most data points. It's counterintuitive, but the spreadsheet bottleneck gets worse under deadline pressure. We helped Idaho Forest Group cut their forecasting from 80+ hours down to 15 hours per cycle, which freed their team to focus on scenario analysis and variance investigation instead of data wrangling. That time shift has a real impact on your close speed and forecast confidence. Quick question: are you the right person to explore how we'd approach this for your planning process? Bradley Milks IBM Account Executive, Data Trust & Planning bradley.milks@ibm.com

emails.subject: How Idaho Forest Group cut forecasting from 80 hours to 15
emails.body: Hi [First Name], I wanted to share something that resonated with a few CFOs I've been talking with. Idaho Forest Group was spending 80+ hours monthly on spreadsheet-based forecasting—pulling historicals, adjusting for seasonality, coordinating across teams. Sound familiar? After implementing IBM Planning Analytics, they cut that down to under 15 hours. Their team went from reactive Excel work to actually analyzing what drives their numbers—market signals, demand patterns, real variables that spreadsheets miss. The kicker? Their forecast accuracy hit 97% benchmark, and they closed quarters faster without sacrificing rigor. I'd love to walk through their full story and show you how this applies to your planning cycle. Would you have 20 minutes next week? Best, Bradley Milks IBM Account Executive, Data Trust & Planning bradley.milks@ibm.com

emails.subject: Quick question on your planning process
emails.body: Hi [First Name], I've reached out a couple times about Planning Analytics—no worries if now isn't the right moment. I just wanted to check: is timing off on your end, or does someone else own the forecasting/planning toolstack? The reason I ask is we're seeing finance teams cut forecasting time from 80+ hours to under 15 hours, which seemed relevant given how much manual work tends to live in spreadsheets during close cycles. Either way, happy to step back. But if you think this could be worth a conversation down the road, just let me know. Bradley Milks IBM Account Executive, Data Trust & Planning bradley.milks@ibm.com

campaigns.name: PA Outreach — VP of IT / CTO

emails.subject: Real-time forecasting (not spreadsheets) for your planning cycle
emails.body: Hi [First Name], I've been working with CTOs in Florida who are stuck in the same boat: fragmented data sources feeding into manual planning processes that take weeks instead of days. You're probably seeing it too—real-time feeds exist, but your forecasting tools can't actually use them. Here's what caught my attention: Idaho Forest Group cut their forecasting from 80+ hours down to 15 by connecting live data and AI automation. Their team went from reactive to predictive. I'm not suggesting you rip out everything tomorrow. But if modernizing your data infrastructure includes making planning faster and less manual, we should talk. Got 15 minutes this week? Bradley Milks IBM Account Executive, Data Trust & Planning bradley.milks@ibm.com

emails.subject: Real-time planning without the manual spreadsheet bottleneck
emails.body: Hi [Name], Following up on my previous note—I wanted to surface something specific to your role: most CTOs we talk to spend cycles just *cleaning data* before their planning teams can even start forecasting. That manual ETL work kills agility when you're trying to connect real-time feeds. We worked with Idaho Forest Group on exactly this. They cut forecast cycles from 80+ hours to under 15 by automating data ingestion and letting AI surface patterns their spreadsheets missed. Planning moved from reactive to predictive. Given your push on data infrastructure modernization, are you the right person to explore how Planning Analytics could plug into your real-time architecture? Open to a brief 15-min call if it lands. Bradley Milks IBM Account Executive, Data Trust & Planning bradley.milks@ibm.com

campaigns.name: PA Outreach — CRO / Chief Revenue Officer

emails.subject: Your revenue forecast vs. what finance actually sees
emails.body: Hi [First Name], I was looking at your company's quarterly performance and got curious about something—most CROs we talk to spend 60+ hours monthly just reconciling forecast data between sales and finance. That gap usually costs them 15-20% forecast accuracy. I work with companies in your space who've cut that reconciliation time dramatically while actually improving their forecast confidence. We helped one forest products company go from 80+ hours of manual forecasting down to under 15, which freed up the team to focus on what actually moves the needle—pipeline strategy and quota planning. Curious if that resonates? Would be worth a quick 15-minute call this week to see if there's something here. Open to your calendar. Bradley Milks IBM Account Executive, Data Trust & Planning bradley.milks@ibm.com

emails.subject: Your forecast accuracy gap (and what it costs quota)
emails.body: Hi [Name], Following up on my note about revenue forecasting—I wanted to flag something specific to your role: most CROs we talk to discover their forecast misses aren't just about sales data quality. They're about *timing*. Sales forecasts quarterly, but finance plans monthly, and nobody has a single source of truth for "what does quota attainment look like next month if we close these deals?" That disconnect alone costs deals. Idaho Forest Group cut their forecasting cycle from 80+ hours to under 15 by connecting their sales pipeline directly into a unified planning model—meaning faster visibility into quota gaps and where to apply capacity. Are you the right person to explore how we could tighten that gap for your team? Bradley Milks IBM Account Executive, Data Trust & Planning bradley.milks@ibm.com

emails.subject: How Idaho Forest Cut Forecasting from 80 Hours to 15
emails.body: Hi [First Name], I wanted to share something relevant to your revenue forecasting work. Idaho Forest Group was struggling with the same challenge—their monthly forecast took 80+ hours across the team, and accuracy suffered because they couldn't incorporate real-time pipeline data fast enough. After implementing IBM Planning Analytics, they cut that down to under 15 hours. More importantly, their forecast accuracy improved because the system automatically surfaced patterns they'd missed in spreadsheets—seasonality, deal velocity shifts, win rate trends. The result? Sales and finance finally had a shared forecast they both trusted, and they could actually see quota attainment risk before month-end. I'd love to walk you through how they structured this. Would a 20-minute call work to explore if this applies to your planning cycle? Best, Bradley Milks IBM Account Executive, Data Trust & Planning bradley.milks@ibm.com

emails.subject: Quick question — is now the wrong time?
emails.body: Hi [Name], I've reached out a couple of times about how IBM Planning Analytics is helping CROs cut forecasting time from weeks to days while nailing accuracy. I know you're busy, so I wanted to check in one more time — either this isn't the right priority for you right now, or maybe someone else on your finance or FP&A team owns the forecasting process? Either way, no pressure. If timing shifts or you want to see how a peer reduced forecast cycles from 80+ hours to under 15 hours, I'm here. Thanks for considering. Bradley Milks IBM Account Executive, Data Trust & Planning bradley.milks@ibm.com

campaigns.name: PA Outreach — Director of Marketing / CMO

emails.body: **Subject: Budget cycles shouldn't take weeks** Hi [First Name], I work with marketing leaders who are stuck in the same cycle: spreadsheets, email chains, delayed approvals, and zero visibility into which campaigns actually drove revenue. Here's what caught my attention—most teams we talk to spend 3-4 weeks just getting budget aligned between marketing and finance. One client cut that to days. The difference? They stopped forecasting in static sheets and started using real data—historical performance, market signals, actual attribution—to model scenarios in minutes instead of days. I think there's a quick conversation worth having about how your team could tighten that cycle and get better budget decisions faster. Open to a 15-minute call this week? Bradley Milks IBM Account Executive, Data Trust & Planning bradley.milks@ibm.com

emails.subject: Re: Your marketing budget cycle – one more thing
emails.body: Hi [Name], Following up on my last note – I realized we didn't touch on something that trips up a lot of CMOs: the gap between what you forecast in marketing and what finance actually approves. Most teams we talk to spend weeks building campaign ROI models in spreadsheets, only to watch them get challenged during budget review because finance is working from different assumptions. Idaho Forest Group cut their forecasting time from 80+ hours to under 15 hours partly because marketing and finance finally had one source of truth. Quick question – when you're modeling next quarter's campaign mix, are you and your finance partner working from the same data set, or does that disconnect slow things down? Bradley Milks IBM Account Executive, Data Trust & Planning bradley.milks@ibm.com

emails.subject: How Idaho Forest Group Cut Campaign Planning from 80+ Hours to 15
emails.body: Hi [First Name], I wanted to share something that resonated with a CMO we worked with recently. Idaho Forest Group faced the same challenge you likely have—their finance and marketing teams operated in silos, making budget approvals slow and ROI attribution nearly impossible. Using IBM Planning Analytics, they compressed their quarterly forecasting cycle from 80+ hours down to 15 hours. More importantly, they unified how marketing spend and finance reviewed the same data, eliminating the back-and-forth that typically delays campaign approvals. The result? They gained real visibility into which campaigns actually drove revenue, and their leadership approved budgets 40% faster. I'd love to walk you through their full story and show how this applies to your team's planning cycles. Are you open to a brief 20-minute call next week? Bradley Milks IBM Account Executive, Data Trust & Planning bradley.milks@ibm.com

emails.subject: Quick question on marketing budget planning
emails.body: Hi [First Name], I've reached out a couple times about how marketing leaders are cutting forecast cycles from weeks to days—and more importantly, getting budget approvals faster because finance actually trusts the numbers. campaigns.name: PA Outreach — CHRO / VP of HR

emails.subject: Headcount forecasting taking weeks instead of days?
emails.body: Hi [First Name], I work with HR leaders who spend 60+ hours monthly rebuilding headcount spreadsheets—only to get forecasts challenged by Finance because the data's already stale. Saw your team manages workforce planning across multiple departments. The challenge I hear most: manual models can't keep pace with hiring approvals, attrition changes, or real-time headcount shifts. We've helped similar organizations cut forecast cycles from 80+ hours down to under 15—giving HR and Finance one shared view that updates automatically. No more spreadsheet reconciliation or approval bottlenecks. Would a quick 15-minute call this week make sense? I can show you how this works for teams like yours. Best, Bradley Milks IBM Account Executive, Data Trust & Planning bradley.milks@ibm.com

emails.subject: Quick follow-up — the hiring approval bottleneck
emails.body: Hi [First Name], Following up on my note last week about headcount forecasting. I wanted to flag something specific I'm hearing from other CHROs: the lag between when finance approves headcount and when HR can actually execute against it — usually because the underlying data lives in different systems. We worked with a forest products company that cut their forecast cycle from 80+ hours to under 15 by connecting their headcount plans directly to actuals and budget data. That freed up their team to handle approvals in days instead of weeks. Are you currently dealing with that approval bottleneck? Would it make sense to grab 15 minutes to talk through how your team manages the handoff between HR and finance? Bradley Milks IBM Account Executive, Data Trust & Planning bradley.milks@ibm.com

emails.subject: How Idaho Forest Cut Headcount Forecasting from 80 Hours to 15
emails.body: Hi [Name], I wanted to share something that might resonate with your team. Idaho Forest Group was spending 80+ hours monthly consolidating headcount data across spreadsheets and getting sign-offs from finance and HR. Their forecasting cycle was eating into strategic planning time. After implementing IBM Planning Analytics, they cut that down to under 15 hours—and gained real-time visibility into labor costs and attrition scenarios across departments. Their team now runs multiple "what-if" plans in minutes instead of days, so hiring approvals actually move forward. Given your focus on workforce planning and labor cost modeling, I think you'd find their approach valuable. Happy to send over the full case study or show you a quick demo of how this could work for your organization. Best, Bradley Milks IBM Account Executive, Data Trust & Planning bradley.milks@ibm.com

emails.subject: Quick question on your forecasting timeline
emails.body: Hi [Name], I know I've reached out a couple times about headcount forecasting—I'll keep this brief. For context: most CHROs we work with are spending 80+ hours annually on manual headcount plans. One client cut that to under 15 hours using Planning Analytics.
